questions.question: What does the '*' wildcard character represent in the ls command?
options: A) Any file name
B) Only directories
C) Only files with the same name
D) Only files with the same owner and group
questions.explanation: The '*' wildcard character represents any file name. It matches zero or more characters.

questions.question: What is the purpose of using '**' in the ls command?
options: A) To match only files with the same name
B) To match only directories
C) To match all files and subdirectories
D) To match only files with the same owner and group
questions.explanation: The '**' wildcard character represents all files and subdirectories.

questions.question: What does the '?' wildcard character represent in the ls command?
options: A) Any character
B) Only one character
C) Any file name
D) Only files with the same name
questions.explanation: The '?' wildcard character represents any single character.

questions.question: What is the purpose of using '[seq]' in the ls command?
options: A) To match only files with the same name
B) To match only directories
C) To match files with names starting with a specified string
D) To match only files with the same owner and group
questions.explanation: The '[seq]' wildcard character pattern matches files with names starting with a specified string.

questions.question: What is an example of a '[a-z]*' pattern?
options: A) Any character between 'a' and 'z'
B) Only files with the same name
C) Only files with the same owner and group
D) Only files with names starting with a lowercase letter
questions.explanation: The '[a-z]*' pattern matches files with names starting with any lowercase letter zero or more times.
